---
title: "Exercises: Module 03 — Remote Repositories"
module: 3
exercise_count: 3
estimated_minutes: 35
platform_tested:
  - macos-14
  - ubuntu-24.04
  - windows-gitbash-2.44
---

## Before You Begin

Complete:

- [Module 03 reading](../modules/03-remotes.qmd)
- [Exercises 02](02-exercises.qmd) — your local repository has at least 3 commits
- SSH key generated and added to GitHub (see your setup guide)

::: {.callout-important}
## Data Safety Reminder
Before pushing anything: ensure your `.gitignore` excludes `data/`, `.env`,
and any files with participant data. Run `git status` and check every file listed.
:::

---

### Exercise 3.1 — Set Up and Verify SSH Authentication

**Goal**: Confirm SSH authentication is working between your terminal and GitHub.

**Steps**:

1. Check if an SSH key exists:

   ```bash
   ls ~/.ssh/
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output (if key exists)

```text
id_ed25519      id_ed25519.pub      known_hosts
```

If you only see `known_hosts` or the directory is empty, generate a key first.
:::

2. If no key exists, generate one:

   ```bash
   ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@institution.edu"
   ```

   Press **Return** to accept defaults. Set a passphrase when prompted.

3. Display your public key:

   ```bash
   cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAI... your@institution.edu
```

Copy this entire line. Add it to GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key.
:::

4. Test the connection:

   ```bash
   ssh -T git@github.com
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output (success)

```text
Hi username! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
```

If you see this, SSH is working. The "no shell access" part is expected and normal.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
**If you get "Permission denied (publickey)"**: Run `ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519` to
load your key into the SSH agent, then try again. If still failing, confirm the
key in GitHub Settings matches the output of `cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`.
:::

---

### Exercise 3.2 — Push Your Repository to GitHub

**Goal**: Create a remote repository and push your local commits to it.

**Setup**: Create an empty repository on GitHub first:
1. Go to github.com → **+** → **New repository**
2. Name: `rehab-research`
3. Leave it **completely empty** (no README, no .gitignore, no license)
4. Copy the SSH URL: `git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git`

**Steps**:

1. Navigate to your local repository:

   ```bash
   cd ~/rehab-research
   git status
   ```

2. Add the remote:

   ```bash
   git remote add origin git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
   git remote -v
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
origin	git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git (push)
```
:::

3. Push your commits:

   ```bash
   git push -u origin main
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
Enumerating objects: 12, done.
Counting objects: 100% (12/12), done.
Delta compression using up to 8 threads
Compressing objects: 100% (8/8), done.
Writing objects: 100% (12/12), 1.02 KiB | 1.02 MiB/s, done.
Total 12 (delta 2), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
 * [new branch]      main -> main
Branch 'main' set up to track remote branch 'main' from 'origin'.
```

`[new branch]` confirms the remote branch was created. "set up to track" means
future `git push` and `git pull` will use this remote automatically.
:::

4. Verify the push worked:

   ```bash
   git log --oneline origin/main
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
b3a2c1d (HEAD -> main, origin/main) Add: .gitignore to exclude data files
1a5f3b2 Add: filter_complete_cases to exclude missing grip strength values
9d4c7e1 Add: normalize_balance function for balance index scores
3f2a1b4 Add: initial analysis script with dplyr and tidyr
```

`origin/main` now appears in the log alongside `main` — they are in sync.
:::

---

### Exercise 3.3 — Trigger and Fix a Rejected Push

**Goal**: Deliberately create a diverged history, encounter the "rejected" error, and fix it.

**Context**: This simulates what happens when a collaborator pushes while you are working.

**Steps**:

1. Make a local commit:

   ```bash
   echo "# Wave 2 analysis placeholder" >> docs/protocol.md
   git add docs/protocol.md
   git commit -m "Docs: add Wave 2 placeholder to protocol"
   ```

2. Use `git commit --amend` to change the commit (this rewrites its hash, creating divergence):

   ```bash
   git commit --amend -m "Docs: Wave 2 data collection protocol placeholder"
   ```

3. Attempt to push:

   ```bash
   git push origin main
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output (the rejected error)

```text
 ! [rejected]        main -> main (non-fast-forward)
error: failed to push some refs to 'git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git'
hint: Updates were rejected because the remote contains work that you do not
hint: have locally. Integrate the remote changes (e.g.
hint: 'git pull --rebase ...') before pushing again.
```

Git is telling you: the remote has commits your local branch does not have.
The hint even tells you the fix: `git pull --rebase`.
:::

4. Fix it with rebase-pull:

   ```bash
   git pull --rebase origin main
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/main.
```

Or if there is a conflict, it will show the conflicting files. For this exercise
there should be none.
:::

5. Push again:

   ```bash
   git push origin main
   ```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Expected Output

```text
Enumerating objects: 5, done.
...
To git@github.com:yourusername/rehab-research.git
   b3a2c1d..c4d5e6f  main -> main
```

Success. The push went through.
:::

**What you learned**:

- Rejected push = remote has commits you do not have locally
- `git pull --rebase origin main` is the standard fix
- `--rebase` replays your commits on top of the fetched commits (cleaner than merge)

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes

- **Using `git push --force`**: This overwrites the remote history and can delete collaborators' work. Never use `--force` on a shared branch. Use `--rebase` instead.
- **`git pull` without `--rebase`**: Creates an unnecessary merge commit. Prefer `--rebase` for keeping history clean.
:::

---

## Summary

You can now:

- Generate SSH keys and authenticate with GitHub
- Add a remote and push with `git push -u origin main`
- Verify remote sync with `git log --oneline origin/main`
- Diagnose and fix a rejected push with `git pull --rebase`

**You are ready for [Module 04 — Branching](../modules/04-branching.qmd).**
